Name: STRESS
LongName: Strengthening the Reporting of Empirical Simulation Studies (STRESS) guidelines
Description: STRESS provides structured reporting checklists for empirical simulation studies to improve clarity, reproducibility, and reuse across discrete-event simulation, agent-based simulation, and system dynamics.
Aim: To strengthen transparent and reproducible reporting of empirical simulation studies by ensuring objectives, model logic, data, experimentation, implementation, and model-sharing details are completely documented.

Scope.Coverage: Empirical simulation manuscripts that build, calibrate, verify, validate, and experiment with DES, ABS, SD, Monte Carlo, queueing, or hybrid simulation models in applied settings.
Scope.Focus: Complete and auditable reporting of simulation objectives, model structure and logic, parameter/data provenance, preprocessing assumptions, scenario design, run configuration (initialisation, warm-up, run length, replications), random sampling controls, implementation environment, and model/code access for reuse.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not applicable to non-simulation uses of the term stress (for example psychological stress, PTSD, stress testing in cardiology, or material/structural stress analyses) unless the manuscript is explicitly an empirical simulation-study report.

primary.description: Monks T, Currie CSM, Onggo BS, et al. Strengthening the reporting of empirical simulation studies: Introducing the STRESS guidelines. Journal of Simulation. 2019;13(1):55-67.

items.item_question: Is the purpose of the simulation model and its intended use clearly reported?
items.item_question_background: A clear purpose frames the model scope and justifies design choices.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the explicit study purpose, modelling rationale, and intended use (experimental frame).

items.item_question: Are the model outputs that will be estimated and analyzed clearly specified?
items.item_question_background: Explicit outputs are required to evaluate whether analyses address study aims.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all primary and secondary model outputs, including definitions and units where available.

items.item_question: Are the aims of simulation experimentation clearly defined?
items.item_question_background: Experiment aims clarify what scenarios or interventions are being compared and why.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the stated objectives of experimentation, including optimization, comparison, sensitivity, or what-if goals.

items.item_question: Is an overview diagram of the base model logic provided?
items.item_question_background: Overview diagrams make complex model structure interpretable and auditable.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether a high-level model diagram is provided and what key model components it includes.

items.item_question: Is the base model logic described in enough detail to understand how the model works?
items.item_question_background: Detailed base logic is required for reproducibility and reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract textual and/or mathematical description of baseline process logic, state transitions, and mechanism flow.

items.item_question: Are differences in logic between baseline and alternative scenarios clearly described?
items.item_question_background: Without clear scenario deltas, it is difficult to attribute output differences to model changes.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract each scenario and precisely what logic changes relative to the base model.

items.item_question: Are key algorithms, rules, or equations used in the model documented?
items.item_question_background: Algorithmic transparency is essential for independent replication.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract algorithms, equations, decision rules, and pseudo-code used in core model logic.

items.item_question: Are model components and their roles clearly described for the simulation paradigm used?
items.item_question_background: Component-level detail anchors reproducibility for DES, ABS, and SD models.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all major components (for example entities/activities/resources, agents/environment/interactions, or stocks/flows/auxiliaries) and their roles.

items.item_question: Are all data sources used for model development and experimentation described?
items.item_question_background: Source transparency is required to assess data quality and representativeness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all input data sources, sample sizes, and acquisition methods.

items.item_question: Are parameter values/distributions and their origins documented?
items.item_question_background: Parameter provenance underpins reproducible execution and sensitivity checks.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract fixed parameters, stochastic distributions, parameter values, and source/estimation approach for each.

items.item_question: Are data preprocessing and transformation steps reported?
items.item_question_background: Preprocessing can materially affect model behaviour and outcomes.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract data-cleaning, transformation, aggregation, and feature-engineering steps prior to simulation.

items.item_question: Are explicit assumptions made during modelling and data preparation documented?
items.item_question_background: Assumptions are often key drivers of model validity and transferability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all substantive modelling and data assumptions, including justifications.

items.item_question: Is model initialization clearly reported?
items.item_question_background: Initialization choices affect early dynamics and output validity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract initial state settings, warm-up procedures (if used), and handling of transient conditions.

items.item_question: Is run length and stopping condition clearly specified?
items.item_question_background: Run length determines whether outcomes are representative and comparable.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract run duration, stopping criteria, and time horizon rationale.

items.item_question: Is the output estimation approach adequately described?
items.item_question_background: Replication and estimation choices determine statistical reliability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract number of replications, summary statistics, confidence intervals, and any variance-reduction approach.

items.item_question: Is the implementation software or programming language clearly identified?
items.item_question_background: Implementation context is needed to recreate execution environment.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract software/platform/language names and version/build details.

items.item_question: Are random-number generation and stochastic sampling procedures reported when relevant?
items.item_question_background: Stochastic reproducibility depends on RNG settings and sampling method.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract pseudo-random number generator details, seeding/stream policy, and sampling method for stochastic elements.

items.item_question: Is model execution control clearly described?
items.item_question_background: Execution policy (for example event processing or integration step) affects results.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract execution approach details such as event scheduling mechanism, time-step/integration settings, and execution workflow.

items.item_question: Are system/hardware specifications and runtime context documented when relevant?
items.item_question_background: Runtime context supports practical reproducibility, especially for larger models.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract hardware and operating environment details and any relevant runtime characteristics.

items.item_question: Is there a clear statement on model/code accessibility?
items.item_question_background: Model sharing statements are central to reproducibility and reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract model/code sharing policy, repository/access path, and any restrictions.

Aliases: Strengthening the Reporting of Empirical Simulation Studies
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We developed a discrete-event simulation of emergency department flow and report model objectives, entity logic, warm-up period, run length, number of replications, and random seed handling.
An agent-based model study evaluates intervention scenarios with explicit base-model logic, scenario logic changes, calibration, verification and validation, and a model-sharing statement.
This system dynamics simulation reports stock-flow structure, parameter sources, preprocessing assumptions, simulation experiment setup, and implementation details needed for replication.
The manuscript compares what-if scenarios in a queueing simulation and provides input parameter distributions, random number generator details, and confidence-interval estimation across replications.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This manuscript studies psychological stress and depression scores in students and does not describe any empirical simulation model.
The paper reports post-traumatic stress disorder prevalence and logistic regression analysis, with no DES, ABS, SD, or simulation experimentation.
This article evaluates cardiac exercise stress tests in patients and includes no simulation model logic, run configuration, or replication setup.
The study investigates tensile and stress-strain properties of composite materials and is not an empirical simulation-study reporting manuscript.
Matching.Explain: Use as a reporting-overlay support guide for empirical simulation manuscripts when DES/ABS/SD or equivalent simulation-experiment language appears in methods/implementation text, even if the paper does not explicitly cite STRESS.
